section.title: Job Replies to Eliphaz
section.sectionRationale: Job answers Eliphaz's gentle counsel with a grief too heavy to be reasoned away. He accuses his friends of failing him like a dried-up desert stream, demands they show him his actual fault rather than assume one, and then, for the first time in the book, turns from talking about God to speaking directly to Him, a shift that will mark every speech Job gives from here on.
section.thesis: Job rejects Eliphaz's easy application of cause and effect, insists his complaint is justified given the weight of his suffering, and turns his grief into the book's first direct address to God, demanding to know why he is watched, tested, and not simply left alone.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the unbearable weight of Job's grief and his wish that God would finish him; his rebuke of his friends as unreliable as a seasonal desert stream; his challenge that they show him his actual error rather than assume one; his reflection on the hard service common to all mortal life; his first direct address to God, refusing to restrain his complaint; and his closing question, why does the watcher of mankind not simply look away.

units.label: The weight of my grief
units.note: the arrows of the Almighty have pierced me

verses.text: Then Job replied:
"If only my grief could be weighed and placed with my calamity on the scales.
For then it would outweigh the sand of the seas; no wonder my words have been rash.

For the arrows of the {{shadday|Almighty}} have pierced me; my spirit drinks in their poison; 
the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
Does a wild donkey bray over fresh grass, or an ox low over its fodder?
Is tasteless food eaten without salt, or is there flavor in the white of an egg?
My soul refuses to touch them; they are loathsome food to me.
If only my request were granted and God would fulfill my hope:
that God would be willing to crush me, to unleash His hand and cut me off!
It still brings me comfort, and joy through unrelenting pain, that I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
What strength do I have, that I should still hope? What is my future, that I should be patient?
Is my strength like that of stone, or my flesh made of bronze?
Is there any help within me now that success is driven from me?

units.label: Faithless as a wadi
units.note: my brothers are as faithless as seasonal streams

verses.text: A despairing man should have the {{chesed|kindness}} of his friend, even if he forsakes the fear of the Almighty.
But my brothers are as faithless as wadis, as seasonal streams that overflow,
darkened because of the ice and the inflow of melting snow,
but ceasing in the dry season and vanishing from their channels in the heat.
Caravans turn aside from their routes; they go into the wasteland and perish.
The caravans of Tema look for water; the travelers of Sheba hope to find it.
They are confounded because they had hoped; their arrival brings disappointment.
For now you are of no help; you see terror, and you are afraid.
Have I ever said, 'Give me something; offer me a bribe from your wealth;
deliver me from the hand of the enemy; redeem me from the grasp of the ruthless'?
Teach me, and I will be silent. Help me understand how I have erred.
How painful are honest words! But what does your argument prove?
Do you intend to correct my words, and treat as wind my cry of despair?
You would even cast lots for an orphan and barter away your friend.
But now, please look at me. Would I lie to your face?
Reconsider; do not be unjust. Reconsider, for my righteousness is at stake.
Is there iniquity on my tongue? Can my mouth not discern malice?

units.label: Man's hard service
units.note: months of futility, nights of misery

verses.text: "Is not man consigned to {{tsaba|labor}} on earth? Are not his days like those of a hired hand?
Like a slave he longs for shade; like a hireling he waits for his wages.
So I am allotted months of futility, and nights of misery are appointed me.
When I lie down I think: 'When will I get up?' But the night drags on, and I toss and turn until dawn.

My flesh is clothed with worms and encrusted with dirt; 
my skin is cracked and festering.
My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle; they come to an end without hope.
Remember that my life is but a breath. My eyes will never again see happiness.
The eye that beholds me will no longer see me. You will look for me, but I will be no more.
As a cloud vanishes and is gone, so he who goes down to Sheol does not come back up.
He never returns to his house; his place remembers him no more.

units.label: Job speaks directly to God
units.note: am I the sea, or the monster of the deep

verses.text:
Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; 
I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
Am I the sea, or the {{tannin|monster of the deep}}, that You must keep me under guard?
When I think my bed will comfort me and my couch will ease my complaint,
then You frighten me with dreams and terrify me with visions,
so that I would prefer strangling and death over my life in this body.
I loathe my life! I would not live forever. Leave me alone, for my days are but a breath.

units.label: What is man, that You test him every moment
units.note: why do You not pardon my transgression

verses.text: What is man that You should exalt him, that You should set Your heart upon him,
that You attend to him every morning, and test him every moment?
Will You never look away from me, or leave me alone to swallow my spittle?
If I have sinned, what have I done to You, O watcher of mankind? Why have You made me Your target, so that I am a burden to You?
Why do You not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For soon I will lie down in the dust; You will seek me, but I will be no more."

shadday.gloss: The Almighty, one of the most frequent divine names in Job. Job uses it here of the very arrows piercing him, the same overwhelming power his friends will invoke to argue God's justice always fits His actions to a person's conduct.

chesed.gloss: Loyal love, steadfast kindness, often used of covenant faithfulness between God and His people, or between friends. Job says a despairing man deserves this from his friend even if he has strayed from proper reverence; the bitter irony is that his own friends withhold exactly this.

tsaba.gloss: Hard service, a term also used for military conscription or compulsory labor. Job describes all human life on earth this way, hard, assigned, wearying, and then describes his own days as an extreme, unrelieved version of that universal toil.

tannin.gloss: A sea monster or dragon, a figure of untamed chaos similar to Leviathan. Job asks God whether he is such a monster, needing to be watched and restrained, a bitter question about why God's attention toward him feels like surveillance of a threat rather than care for a person.

basic.context: Job answers Eliphaz by insisting his grief is heavier than the sand of the seas and that his rash words are understandable given the weight of what he carries. He rebukes his friends for failing him like a desert stream that promises water and delivers none, and challenges them to actually show him where he has erred instead of assuming he must have. He then reflects on the hard, wearying service common to all human life, and for the first time in the book turns to address God directly, asking why he is watched and tested every moment rather than simply left alone to die in peace.
basic.hermeneutics: This speech shows that lament and protest can coexist with genuine faith; Job says he has not denied the words of the Holy One even while wishing for death and accusing his friends of failure. His demand, teach me where I have erred, is a fair one given what the reader already knows, and his shift to addressing God directly marks real spiritual progress, even though the content of what he says to God is raw complaint rather than composed praise.
basic.originalLanguages: Job says a suffering man deserves chesed, loyal kindness, from his friend, and finds none. He describes ordinary human existence as tsaba, hard conscripted service, then intensifies that picture with his own unrelieved suffering. And he asks God whether he is a tannin, a sea monster, that needs to be watched and restrained, turning the language of cosmic chaos into a personal, wounded question.
basic.typology: Job's demand to know his specific fault, met with silence, anticipates every innocent sufferer's cry for an explanation that does not come on schedule. His turn to direct address of God, even in anger and complaint, models the kind of honest prayer that the Psalms will make a permanent feature of Israel's worship.
basic.christ: Job asks why God will not simply pardon his transgression and let him be; Christ is the answer to exactly that longing, securing pardon for real transgression through His own suffering, not by God looking away but by God Himself bearing the cost. Job's cry, why have You made me Your target, finds its deepest answer in a cross where God's own Son became, in a far greater sense, the target of judgment so that pardon could be given freely.
basic.god: God is presented here as relentlessly attentive, testing man every moment, which Job experiences as suffocating surveillance rather than comfort. The tension between God's care and God's apparent harshness is not resolved in this chapter; it is simply voiced, honestly, as a real question asked directly to God rather than merely about Him.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 8:4 asks a strikingly similar question, what is man that You are mindful of him, but in wonder rather than complaint; Lamentations 3:1-20 gives voice to comparable raw lament; Romans 8:33-34 answers Job's demand for pardon with the assurance that no one can bring a charge against God's elect.
basic.oneStory: Job's turn to direct address of God, however raw, is a hinge in the book: suffering that was spoken about now becomes suffering spoken to God. That shift, and Job's unanswered longing for pardon, anticipates the gospel's answer, a Mediator who both hears every honest complaint and secures the pardon Job could only ask for.

context.literary: This is Job's first reply in the dialogue cycle, structured as a direct rebuttal of Eliphaz followed by a decisive shift, at 7:11, from speaking about his situation to speaking directly to God, a pattern that will recur and deepen in Job's later speeches.
context.historical: The image of a wadi, a desert stream that roars with meltwater in spring and vanishes in the dry season, would have been vivid to any hearer familiar with the seasonal water sources of the ancient Near East, and it becomes Job's sharpest image yet for friends whose sympathy dries up exactly when needed most.
context.canonical: Job's language here anticipates the raw honesty of individual lament psalms; his willingness to address God directly even in bitter complaint stands within a larger biblical tradition of prayer that does not require composure before it can be spoken.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show a sufferer's legitimate protest against both false friends and an apparently relentless God, without that protest constituting the sin the friends will keep assuming it is.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's raw complaint and his demand for an explanation; it does not prescribe wishing for death as normative, nor does it settle every theological question Job raises. What it does model as abiding truth is that honest complaint addressed to God, even angry complaint, is a legitimate form of faith rather than its abandonment.

debates.question: Is Job's wish for death in this chapter sinful despair, or legitimate lament?

views.label: Legitimate lament within the bounds of faith
views.case: Job explicitly says he has not denied the words of the Holy One, verse 10, even while wishing for death; the narrator has not reversed his earlier verdicts that Job did not sin.

views.label: The edge of what faith can bear, without yet crossing into sin
views.case: The intensity of Job's language, wishing God would crush him, sets up the later divine rebuke for words that darken counsel without knowledge; this chapter may be the beginning of that drift, even if it does not itself cross the line.

debates.bearing: Whichever reading is preferred, the chapter treats Job's raw honesty as an ongoing, legitimate part of his faith rather than settling the question of whether he ever goes too far.

study.originalLanguages: Job appeals to chesed, loyal kindness, that friends owe one another, and finds it absent. He names ordinary life tsaba, hard conscripted service, before describing his own worse case, and asks God whether he is a tannin, sea monster, requiring constant watch, turning cosmic chaos imagery into a deeply personal complaint about feeling surveilled rather than loved.

typology.type: Job's demand for pardon of a transgression he cannot even identify
typology.fulfillment: The pardon secured at the cross, where Christ bears the full weight of transgression so that God's people receive forgiveness they could never locate or earn on their own.

typology.type: Job's turn from speaking about God to speaking directly to God in complaint
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of honest lament fulfilled and perfected in the Psalms, and ultimately in Christ's own cry from the cross.

study.christ: Job asks why God will not simply pardon his transgression and leave him alone; the gospel answers that longing not by God looking away but by God drawing near in the person of His Son to bear the transgression Himself. Where Job feels watched like a caged sea monster, Christ was watched, tried, and executed as though He were the true source of chaos and guilt, though He had none, so that those who deserve such watching could be released into peace.
study.god: God appears here as relentlessly attentive, testing man every moment, in a way Job experiences as suffocating rather than comforting. The chapter does not resolve whether this attention is fatherly care or something harsher; it simply gives voice to how crushing suffering can make even God's faithfulness feel like a threat, without condemning Job for saying so.

crossReferences.note: What is man that You are mindful of him, echoing Job's question in 7:17 but framed in wonder rather than complaint.
A comparably raw lament over affliction, showing Job's honesty here fits a wider pattern of legitimate biblical complaint.
No one can bring a charge against God's elect, answering Job's longing for pardon with the assurance secured in Christ.

study.oneStory: Job's first reply moves from rebuking his friends to addressing God directly, a hinge that turns suffering spoken about into suffering spoken to God. His unanswered longing for pardon and rest anticipates the gospel's fuller answer: a Mediator who hears every honest complaint and who Himself secures, at great cost, the pardon Job could only long for.

sermon.title: Miserable Comforters
sermon.why: Keller preaches on Job 5:1-7 and 6:1-10 together, examining the bad comfort of the friends and the better and ultimate comfort found only in Christ, directly addressing Job's rebuke of his friends as faithless as a dried-up desert stream.

quiz.q: Why does Job say his 'words have been rash' in 6:2-3?

options.text: Because his grief and calamity, if weighed, would outweigh the sand of the seas, making his intense words understandable given the scale of his suffering.
options.note: Job offers this as an explanation, not an excuse, for the intensity of his earlier lament in chapter 3.

options.text: Because the arrows of the Almighty have pierced him so deeply that his spirit itself drinks in their poison.
options.note: A true and vivid detail from the same passage, though less precise than his direct comparison of his grief to the weight of the sand of the seas.

options.text: Because he regrets rebuking his friends.
options.note: Job is about to rebuke his friends further, not retract an earlier rebuke; this misreads the sequence of the speech.

options.text: Because he now admits he was entirely wrong to speak at all.
options.note: Job defends the legitimacy of his complaint rather than retracting it.

quiz.q: What image does Job use to describe his friends' failure to comfort him?

options.text: A wadi, a seasonal desert stream that overflows with meltwater but vanishes exactly when travelers need water most.
options.note: This extended image in 6:15-20 captures how his friends' sympathy has proven unreliable when it mattered most.

options.text: A stream that promises relief but disappoints thirsty travelers when they finally reach it.
options.note: Captures the heart of the image, though less specific than naming the seasonal wadi and its meltwater origin directly.

options.text: A lion that roars but has no teeth.
options.note: This lion image appears earlier in Eliphaz's speech, 4:10-11, not in Job's rebuke of his friends here.

options.text: A well that never runs dry.
options.note: Job's image is the opposite, a stream that fails, not one that endures reliably.

quiz.q: What specific challenge does Job put to his friends in 6:24-30?

options.text: To actually teach him where he has erred, rather than simply assume guilt, since he insists his righteousness is at stake.
options.note: Job demands evidence, not assumption, echoing the reader's own knowledge that no such specific fault exists.

options.text: To stop treating his cry of despair as mere wind and actually engage his specific argument.
options.note: A true part of Job's challenge, though less precise than his direct demand that they identify his actual error.

options.text: To agree that all suffering is meaningless.
options.note: Job's challenge is specifically about identifying his own fault, not a broader claim about the meaninglessness of suffering.

options.text: To leave immediately and never return.
options.note: Job wants them to answer his challenge, not simply depart; he continues to engage them in dialogue for many more chapters.

quiz.q: What significant shift happens at Job 7:11?

options.text: Job turns from speaking about his situation to speaking directly to God for the first time in the book.
options.note: This marks a real development in Job's posture, moving from complaint about circumstances to direct, if raw, address of God Himself.

options.text: Job begins openly questioning why God watches him so closely, addressing his complaint toward God rather than only about Him.
options.note: Captures the substance of the shift, though less precise than naming it explicitly as Job's first direct address to God in the book.

options.text: The narrator interrupts with commentary on Job's words.
options.note: There is no narrator interruption here; the shift is within Job's own speech, from talking about God to talking to Him.

options.text: Job stops speaking entirely for the rest of the book.
options.note: Job continues to speak extensively throughout the following chapters.

quiz.q: What does Job's closing question in 7:20-21 reveal about his state of mind?

options.text: He longs for pardon and release, asking why God does not simply forgive whatever fault exists and leave him alone before he dies.
options.note: This closing plea combines Job's continued uncertainty about any specific fault with a genuine longing for mercy and rest.

options.text: He wishes God would simply stop testing him every moment and let him have a measure of peace before death.
options.note: A true part of Job's closing plea, though less complete than his explicit request for pardon of whatever iniquity exists.

options.text: He has abandoned belief that God hears him.
options.note: Job's direct address to God assumes God is listening even as he complains; this is not a statement of unbelief.

options.text: He is confident and at peace about his situation.
options.note: The chapter's tone throughout is one of anguish and complaint, not confidence or peace.

questions: Job says a suffering person deserves the loyal kindness, chesed, of a friend even if that person has strayed. Have you ever withheld kindness from someone because you first wanted to assess whether they deserved it?

Job compares his friends to a desert stream that promises water and delivers none exactly when it is needed most. Where have you disappointed someone in their hour of real need, even with good intentions?

For the first time in the book, Job turns from talking about God to talking directly to Him, even in complaint. What keeps you from bringing your rawest thoughts directly to God in prayer, rather than just thinking or talking about your situation?
Job demands evidence rather than assumption from his friends: teach me where I have erred. Where might you be assuming the cause of someone's suffering rather than actually asking or listening?

Job ends this speech longing simply to be pardoned and left in peace. What would it look like for you to bring an unresolved ache honestly before God this week, without needing to have the full explanation first?

translationNotes.note: Kindness renders chesed, a rich covenantal term for loyal love; Job's point is sharpened by the fact that this is exactly what his friends are failing to provide.
Labor renders tsaba, a term also used of military service or hard conscripted duty, casting all human life, not only Job's, as a kind of assigned hardship.

Monster of the deep renders tannin, a sea-dragon figure of chaos similar to Leviathan and Rahab elsewhere in the Old Testament; Job's question personalizes this cosmic image into a complaint about feeling watched like a threat rather than loved as a person.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 6:1-7:21. The em dash in verse 6:3 of the source text was replaced with a semicolon per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
